text:
Admission Date:  1982-11-01              Discharge Date:   1974-11-17

Date of Birth:  2015-09-22             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions

Attending:Tracy
Chief Complaint:
SOB, weight gain

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Transesophageal echocardiogram
DC cardioversion


History of Present Illness:
 82 yo man w/ hx of CHF (EF 40-45% 2009-02-17), NSTEMI on 2011-07-15,
afib on coumadin and COPD who presents from home with weight
gain and worsening SOB. Per the patient's son, pt was seen in
cardiology clinic yesterday and was supposed to have undergone
cardioversion, but had a subtheraputic INR. Over the past 2
weeks he has been having steady weight gain now 12 lbs above dry
weight (dry wt 187 lbs at last d/c last month) in the setting of
increased fluid intake. Also has been using home O2 all day
instead of just QHS for the past 2 days. He has not been having
CP. States SOB has been constant, he states he gets winded going
up a flight of stairs at home. He has a chronic cough that is
unchanged. He sleeps on one pillow with a blanket under at
night. States he is able to lie flat without increased symptoms.

He lives at home with son and wife, his son notes he found a
pack of cigs a few weeks ago that was missing 6 cigs (son feels
pt was smoking though the pt denies). Son also notes the pt has
been trying to follow a low sodium / fluid restricted diet, but
he has been drinking more fluids recently.
Saw PCP yesterday and cardiologist earlier this month. Have been
considering cardioversion for his A Fib but his INR has been
subtherapeutic. PCP increased his lasix to 80mg PO daily
yesterday.
Pt denies F/C/S, abd pain, change in bowel habits. He does not
like hospitalizations.
.
In the ED, initial vitals were 96.6 62 119/74 24 96% RA.
Labs and imaging significant for INR 1.8, Cr 2.0 (baseline
1.5-2.0)
Patient given an albuterol nebulizer and 80mg IV lasix.
Vitals on transfer were 96.5, 65, 110/68, 20 100% 2L.
.
Cardiac review of systems is notable for absence of chest pain,
paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, palpitations, syncope
or presyncope.


Past Medical History:
COPD
Coronary artery disease (status post NSTEMI)
ischemic cardiomyopathy
Congestive heart failure
Atrial Fibrillation
Severe Mitral Regurgitation
Hypertension.
Hyperlipidemia.
Tobacco abuse.
Borderline obesity.

Social History:
He is married. He lives with his wife and son in Jeffery Flores. He is
a retired supervisor of the dishwashing department at [**University/College **]. Currently trying to quit smoking, but smoking
intermittently currently. He previously smoked a half a pack of
cigarettes daily for the past 50 years. He does not drink
alcohol or use illicit substances, but did drink when he was
younger.


Family History:
His father died in his 60s of a myocardial infarction. His
mother died at age [**Age over 90 **] of Alzheimer's. He has two brothers, three
sisters, and three sons. One sister has hypertension and one son
has hyperlipidemia. There is no family history notable for
stroke, diabetes, or early coronary artery disease or sudden
cardiac death.

Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM
VS: T=97.6BP=126/81HR=65RR=20O2 sat= 96% 2L
GENERAL: WDWN M in NAD. Oriented x3. Mood, affect appropriate.
HEENT: NCAT. Sclera anicteric. PERRL, EOMI. Conjunctiva were
pink, no pallor or cyanosis of the oral mucosa. No xanthalesma.

NECK: Supple with JVD to mandible
CARDIAC: Irregular rhythm, normal S1 and S2 with a soft 10-26
holosystolic murmur at the apex, no rubs or gallops.
LUNGS: No chest wall deformities, scoliosis or kyphosis. Resp
were unlabored, no accessory muscle use. B/l rales diffusely
with course breath sounds, no egophony.
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness.
EXTREMITIES: No c/c. B/l pitting edema 1+ to the knee.
SKIN: No stasis dermatitis, ulcers, scars, or xanthomas.
PULSES:
Right: DP 2+
Left: DP 2+

DISCHARGE EXAM

O2 sat= 97%RA
LOS: -6L;
WT: 89.5kg --> 88.5kg --> 89.1 --> 87.5 --> 87.3kg
GENERAL: WDWN Man in NAD Oriented x3. Mood, affect appropriate.
HEENT: NCAT. Sclera anicteric. PERRL, EOMI. Conjunctiva were
pink, no pallor or cyanosis of the oral mucosa. No xanthalesma.
NECK: Supple with JVD difficult to assess.
CARDIAC: RRR, normal S1 and S2 with a soft 10-26 holosystolic
murmur at the apex, no rubs or gallops.
LUNGS: No chest wall deformities, scoliosis or kyphosis.
Breathing unlabored.  Few crackles at the bases.
ABDOMEN: Soft, NTND. No HSM or tenderness.
EXTREMITIES: No c/c. B/l pitting edema trace-1+ to the knee.
SKIN: No stasis dermatitis, ulcers, scars, or xanthomas.

Reports:
CXR: Upright AP and lateral chest radiographs demonstrate low
lung
volumes. Hazy indistinctness of the pulmonary vessels may
indicate mild
edema, particularly in light of given history. No pleural
effusion or
pneumothorax is noted. The heart size is moderately enlarged and
there is
tortuosity of the aorta, both exaggerated by the low lung
volumes.

TEE:The left atrium is mildly dilated. No spontaneous echo
contrast or thrombus is seen in the body of the left atrium/left
atrial appendage or the body of the right atrium/right atrial
appendage. The left atrial appendage has an accessory lobe
(normal variant). The left atrial appendage emptying velocity is
depressed (<0.2m/s). Right atrial appendage ejection velocity is
good (>20 cm/s). A small color Doppler signal of left-to-right
flow across the interatrial septum is seen at rest consistent
with a small secundum atrial septal defect. Overall left
ventricular systolic function is mildly depressed. Right
ventricle with borderline normal free wall function. There are
extensive simple atheroma in the descending thoracic aorta. The
aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly thickened. No aortic
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. Moderate to severe (3+) mitral regurgitation is seen.
The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly thickened.
IMPRESSION: Small secundum type ASD with left-to-right shunt at
rest. No spontaneous echo contrast or thrombus in the
LA/LAA/RA/RAA. Moderate to severe mitral regurgitation.
Extensive, simple aortic atheroma.

TTE:
The left atrium is moderately dilated. The right atrium is
moderately dilated. There is color Doppler evidence of a small
interatrial shunt (stretched patent foramen ovale).There is mild
symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy. The left ventricular
cavity size is top normal/borderline dilated. There is mild to
moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with
basal to mid inferior akinesis/hypokinesis, Smith inferoseptal
akinesis/hypokinesis and basal to mid inferolateral akinesis.
[Intrinsic left ventricular systolic function is likely more
depressed given the severity of valvular regurgitation.] The
right ventricular cavity is mildly dilated with normal free wall
contractility. [Intrinsic right ventricular systolic function is
likely more depressed given the severity of tricuspid
regurgitation.] The aortic valve leaflets (3) are mildly
thickened. There is no aortic valve stenosis. No aortic
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly
thickened. Moderate to severe (3+) mitral regurgitation is seen.
The tricuspid valve leaflets are mildly thickened. Severe [4+]
tricuspid regurgitation is seen. There is moderate pulmonary
artery systolic hypertension. [In the setting of at least
moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation, the estimated
pulmonary artery systolic pressure may be underestimated due to
a very high right atrial pressure.] There is no pericardial
effusion.

Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of 2009-02-17,
biventricular systolic function appears similar. Mtiral
regurgitation appears similar. Tricuspid regurgitation is now
more prominent. Estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure is
now higher.
.
07-22 CXR: FINDINGS: Upright PA and lateral views of the chest
show improvement in the mild pulmonary edema. There has been a
decrease in the left pleural effusion with better definition of
the left hemidiaphragm. Cardiac silhouette remains enlarged. No
pneumothorax.
 IMPRESSION: Decrease in moderate pulmonary edema after
diuresis.
.

K-3.6 Cl-102 HCO3-37* AnGap-10
1974-11-17 06:45AM BLOOD Calcium-9.0 Phos-4.4 Mg-2.3

Brief Hospital Course:
Healthsouth Rehabilitation Hospital Of Wichita Falls Course #1:
82 yo man w/ hx of CHF (EF 40-45% 2009-02-17), NSTEMI on 2011-07-15,
afib on coumadin and COPD who presents from home with weight
gain and worsening SOB c/w acute CHF exacerbation.  Intially was
diuresed, and given standing ipratropium nebs.  He had per the
son started smoking again, and the negative impacts of smoking
on all aspects of his health was stressed with the patient.  It
was decided to pursue TEE/DCCV, with the premise that atrial
kick would improve forward flow and decrease his rather high
rate of decompensated heart failure admissions.

CCU Course:
On the day of transfer, patient was cardioverted with TEE, as it
was felt that patient's poor forward flow might be improved with
sinus rhythm. Post cardioversion he was noted to be hypotensive
and bradycardic with what appeared to be a junctional rhythm.
He was started on a dopamine gtt and transferred to the CCU. His
carvedilol, amiodarone and diltiazem were held. His pressure and
HR improved and he was weaned off of dopamine and transferred
back to Healthsouth Rehabilitation Hospital Of Wichita Falls.

Healthsouth Rehabilitation Hospital Of Wichita Falls Course #2:
Patient came back with SBPs in 120s, HR in 60s, on 2L with sats
100%.  His oxygen was weaned off during the day (pt uses home O2
2L qhs).  Diuresis was continued with Kaiser Fnd Hospital - Moreno Valley lasix 80mg IV, and Kaiser Fnd Hospital - Moreno Valley
electrolytes.  His blood pressures remained in the 110s-120s
systolic with HR in 50s-60s for 36-48 hours post DCCV.
Overnight 2006-12-27, his pressures began to increase, as did his
heart rate.  Carvedilol was added as the BP and HR tolerated,
and Valsartan was added as the BP allowed.  Spironolactone was
also added given the degree of heart failure.  Albuterol was
started given the degree of COPD with great response - he was
instructed not to use more than twice daily given the concern
for it contributing the patient flipping back into atrial
fibrillation.  He was strongly encouraged not to smoke, and to
limit his salt and water intake.  This was stressed multiple
times to him and his family.  His lasix was changed to 120mg PO
daily in the morning, and he was instructed to follow-up
closely.

Transitional issues:
Now in sinus
Still with severe MR
COPD may have worsened - pt may be sneaking smokes on occasion
Diltizem was stopped.  Carvedilol was decreased, warfarin was
increased, albuterol was started (twice daily max - don't want
to flip him back into afib), lasix was increased, spironolactone
was started.

Medications on Admission:
AMIODARONE - 200 mg Tablet - 1 Tablet(s) by mouth daily
ATORVASTATIN [LIPITOR] - 80 mg Tablet - one Tablet(s) by mouth
nightly at hour of sleep

inhaled via nebulizer machine daily

times
daily
FUROSEMIDE - 40 mg Tablet - two Tablet(s) by mouth every morning

for three days, then decrease to 1 tablet per day. If weight
increases by 2 pounds, take an additional 10-19 tablet (20 mg)
that
day.
IPRATROPIUM BROMIDE - 0.2 mg/mL (0.02 %) Solution - one vial
inhaled by nebulizer machine four times daily as needed for
shortness of breath, wheezing
IRBESARTAN [AVAPRO] - 150 mg Tablet - one Tablet(s) by mouth
daily

sublingually x1 as needed for chest pain call 911
WARFARIN - 1 mg Tablet - 2 Tablet(s) by mouth Once Daily at 4 PM

ASPIRIN - (OTC) - 81 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) - one
Tablet(s) by mouth daily

Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
Sycamore Medical Center Homecare

Discharge Diagnosis:
Acute congestive heart failure
Severe mitral regurgitiation
Pulmonary Artery hypertension
Atrial fibrillation
COPD

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted for heart failure.  Your weight had increased,
as did your oxygen requirement.  You were given medications to
get rid of extra fluid.  You also had a procedure to put your
heart into a regular rhythm which was done because your
cardiologists thought it would help your heart and lead to less
extra fluid.

After the procedure, your heart rate was low and your blood
pressure was low, so you went to the intensive care unit for
close monitoring.  Your blood pressure and heart rate improved,
and you came back to the regular cardiology unit.  We slowly
added back medications for your high blood pressure and high
heart rate for their benefits on their heart.

You should make absolutely sure to watch what you eat and drink,
and eat NO MORE THAN 2 GRAMS OF SODIUM PER DAY (salt).  You
should also not drink any more than 1.5 liters of fluid per day
(which is the same as about 4 cans of soda per day).  The
nutritionist has seen you in the hospital on several occasions,
and you should make sure to follow the recommendations.

Doing so will help PREVENT FUTURE HOSPITALIZATIONS, something
you have stated you would like to do many times.

You have several follow-up appointments.  It is very important
to keep this appointments.  On Monday, you are scheduled to see
Dr. Amber.  He is an expert on your condition (heart failure)
and you are likely to benefit very much from seeing him - please
make sure to keep this appointment!

Weigh yourself every morning, call your doctor if your weight
goes up more than 3 lbs.

We have made the following medication changes:

Please START taking:
-Spironolactone 25mg daily

-Coumadin 2.5mg daily and rechek your INR no later than Monday
with your Care Group VNA 673-855-7350

-Carvedilol 6.25mg daily (this is a DECREASE in your previous
dose).

-Furosemide (lasix) 120mg once daily - this is an INCREASE in
your previous dose, you should take this dose until you see Dr.
Amber on Monday.

-Albuterol nebulization no more than two times per day as needed
for shortness of breath or wheezing.

Please STOP taking:
-Diltiazem
